instructions: Choose the correct heading for each paragraph. There are more headings than paragraphs.

headings.text: Turning neglected sites into useful shared spaces
Adapting cultivation to the limits of city environments
Gardens as places for practical community learning
Digital tools that improve everyday growing decisions
The rapid replacement of conventional agriculture
Commercial pressure to close successful gardens

paragraphs.text: Many early urban gardens were not created as part of a formal food policy. Residents began planting on abandoned lots because the land attracted rubbish and made neighbourhoods feel unsafe. Turning these sites into shared gardens improved their appearance while providing herbs and vegetables close to home. The harvest was useful, but participants often valued the return of regular activity to an empty space just as highly. In this way, food production became one part of a broader effort to repair neglected surroundings.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph mainly explains how unused and unsafe land was transformed into active, useful community space.

paragraphs.text: Growing crops in a city requires methods that differ from those used on large rural farms. Shallow rooftop soil cannot support every plant, nearby buildings may block sunlight, and water must often be carried or pumped to the site. Gardeners respond by choosing compact varieties, collecting rainwater, and placing containers where light is strongest. Some use vertical frames to increase the growing area without occupying more ground. These solutions do not remove every limitation, but they make productive use of restricted urban space.
paragraphs.explanation: The focus is on practical ways gardeners adjust to limited soil, light, water, and space.

paragraphs.text: Community gardens increasingly serve as outdoor classrooms rather than simply places to collect food. Schools may use a small plot to demonstrate plant growth, insects, or composting, while older residents share knowledge about seasonal planting. New gardeners learn by watching experienced volunteers and testing techniques for themselves. Because the work produces visible results, lessons about nutrition and ecology can feel less abstract. The central benefit described by participants is not a larger harvest but the exchange of knowledge between people of different ages and backgrounds.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph emphasizes education and the exchange of practical knowledge in community gardens.

paragraphs.text: A newer generation of city gardens uses inexpensive sensors to guide routine care. Small devices can report when soil is dry, while online weather data helps coordinators decide whether watering is necessary. Shared calendars also prevent several volunteers from completing the same task. Such systems do not replace the judgement of an experienced gardener, especially when plants show unusual damage. Their main value is that they organize basic information and help teams respond consistently when volunteers visit the site at different times.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph describes how sensors, weather data, and calendars support routine garden management.

passageTitle: How Sleep Became an Active Research Field
instructions: Choose the correct heading for each paragraph. There are more headings than paragraphs.

headings.text: A major change in the scientific view of sleep
New methods and the limits of measuring sleep
The connection between sleep and remembered learning
Why individual differences complicate general conclusions
Dream content as a complete record of daily experience
The case for identical school schedules worldwide

paragraphs.text: For much of the nineteenth century, many researchers treated sleep as a passive period in which the brain simply reduced its activity. This interpretation changed when electrical recordings showed that the sleeping brain moves through recognizable stages. Some stages contain slow waves, while others display patterns closer to waking activity. Sleep therefore came to be viewed as an organized biological process rather than an absence of consciousness. That shift encouraged scientists to ask what each stage contributes to health, learning, and emotional regulation.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph centers on the historical shift from viewing sleep as passive to viewing it as an organized process.

paragraphs.text: Modern sleep laboratories combine several measurements, including brain activity, eye movement, breathing, and muscle tension. These signals allow researchers to identify sleep stages with reasonable accuracy. Portable watches can gather data from people at home for longer periods, although they estimate sleep mainly from movement and heart rate. Laboratory equipment offers greater detail but may make participants sleep less naturally. Researchers must therefore choose between precise observation in an unfamiliar setting and less exact information collected during ordinary life.
paragraphs.explanation: The main idea is the trade-off between detailed laboratory measurement and less precise home monitoring.

paragraphs.text: A consistent finding is that sleep after study can improve later recall. During sleep, recently learned information appears to be reorganized and connected with existing knowledge. Experiments have shown benefits for facts, physical skills, and some kinds of problem solving, although the strongest stage may differ by task. Sleep does not create knowledge that was never learned, nor does it guarantee perfect memory. Instead, it seems to strengthen selected traces that were formed while a person was awake.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph explains how sleep supports the consolidation of information learned while awake.

paragraphs.text: Simple recommendations about sleep are difficult because people do not respond in exactly the same way. Age, health, daily timing, and previous sleep loss can all influence an experimental result. A schedule that improves performance for one group may have little effect on another. Even within the same person, the amount of sleep needed can change during illness or intense study. Researchers increasingly report these differences instead of presenting a single number as a universal requirement for everyone.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph focuses on individual variation and why it prevents one universal conclusion about sleep needs.

instructions: Choose the correct heading for each paragraph. There are more headings than paragraphs.

headings.text: Why variable generation creates a storage need
An established solution restricted by geography
Rapid battery progress accompanied by material concerns
Combining storage types through coordinated management
The immediate end of all fossil-fuel generation
How every household can disconnect from the grid

paragraphs.text: Wind turbines and solar panels generate electricity without burning fuel, but their output follows weather and daylight rather than demand. A sunny afternoon may produce more power than homes can use, while a calm evening can leave the same system short. Electricity networks must balance supply and demand continuously to avoid instability. Storage provides one way to move energy from periods of surplus to periods of need. Its importance grows as variable renewable sources supply a larger share of the grid.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph explains the mismatch between renewable generation and demand that creates the need for storage.

paragraphs.text: Pumped hydroelectric storage has supported electricity systems for decades. When power is plentiful, water is pumped from a lower reservoir to a higher one. It is released through turbines when electricity is needed again. The method can store large amounts of energy for many hours and the equipment often lasts for generations. However, suitable sites require major differences in elevation and access to water. Environmental approval can also be difficult, so the technology cannot simply be built wherever demand is highest.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph presents pumped hydro as a proven large-scale method whose use is limited by site requirements.

paragraphs.text: Lithium-based batteries have fallen in price and can be installed quickly near renewable generators or urban demand. Their fast response makes them useful for correcting short changes in supply. Yet manufacturing depends on minerals that require energy to extract and are concentrated in a limited number of regions. Recycling systems are improving, but they do not yet recover every material economically. Researchers are therefore developing alternative chemistries while trying to extend the working life of existing battery packs.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph balances the speed and falling cost of battery deployment against mineral and recycling concerns.

paragraphs.text: No single storage technology performs every job at the lowest cost. Batteries respond quickly, pumped systems hold large reserves, and heat storage can serve buildings or industry directly. Grid operators can combine these options and use software to decide when each should charge or release energy. This approach treats storage as a coordinated portfolio rather than a competition with one winner. Careful management can reduce waste and reserve expensive technologies for the tasks where their particular strengths matter most.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph advocates coordinating several storage methods according to their different strengths.

instructions: Choose the correct heading for each paragraph. There are more headings than paragraphs.

headings.text: Using movement through space to shape a visitor's story
Balancing visual atmosphere with the protection of objects
Flexible rooms for collections that continue to change
Designing access for different bodies and ways of learning
Why higher ticket prices improve exhibition quality
Replacing all historic museum buildings with new ones

paragraphs.text: Museum layout influences how visitors understand an exhibition before they read a single label. A narrow entrance may create suspense, while an open central space helps people compare objects from several directions. Designers also decide whether a route should be chronological, thematic, or freely explored. Clear orientation matters because visitors who feel lost spend more energy finding the exit than considering the collection. Movement is therefore used as a narrative tool, guiding attention without requiring everyone to follow exactly the same path.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph explains how spatial routes and movement help communicate an exhibition's narrative.

paragraphs.text: Light gives galleries atmosphere and allows visitors to notice colour and texture, but it can also cause permanent damage. Paper, fabric, and some pigments fade after repeated exposure, even when the change is initially invisible. Conservation teams set limits for sensitive objects and may rotate them off display. Architects respond with filtered windows, shaded areas, and carefully aimed lamps. The goal is not maximum brightness; it is enough light for meaningful viewing without shortening the life of the collection.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph focuses on the trade-off between effective lighting and long-term conservation.

paragraphs.text: Permanent walls can become a problem when museums acquire new objects or change their interpretation of a collection. Many recent galleries use movable partitions, raised floors, and adjustable display systems. A room can then support a large sculpture one year and a group of small cases the next. Flexibility has limits, since heavy objects and climate-controlled cases still need specialist support. Nevertheless, adaptable spaces reduce the need for expensive rebuilding whenever curators develop a different exhibition plan.
paragraphs.explanation: The main idea is creating adaptable gallery spaces for changing collections and exhibition plans.

paragraphs.text: Accessibility extends beyond providing a ramp at the entrance. Seating at regular intervals helps visitors with limited stamina, while varied label heights serve both standing adults and wheelchair users. Audio description, tactile models, and quieter rooms offer additional ways to engage with a collection. These features benefit more people than their original target groups; for example, a clear audio guide can help someone reading in a second language. Inclusive design therefore expands how information can be encountered throughout the museum.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph describes inclusive physical and informational design for varied needs and learning preferences.

instructions: Choose the correct heading for each paragraph. There are more headings than paragraphs.

headings.text: The ecological case for limiting extraction
Why the position and shape of protection matter
Local support as a foundation for effective enforcement
Using repeated evidence to adjust management
Tourism income as the only measure of success
Replacing wild fisheries entirely with aquaculture

paragraphs.text: Marine protected areas are often created after fishing pressure has reduced the number or average size of important species. Limiting extraction gives adult animals more opportunities to reproduce and allows damaged habitats to recover. Protection does not produce immediate results, especially for slow-growing species, but well-managed areas can develop larger breeding populations over time. Some young fish may later move beyond the boundary. The central ecological argument is therefore that reducing local pressure can rebuild biological capacity rather than merely preserve a fixed snapshot.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph explains the ecological reasons for restricting fishing and allowing populations to recover.

paragraphs.text: A boundary drawn neatly on a map may fail if it ignores how species actually use the sea. Some fish move between feeding and breeding grounds, while larvae travel with currents. A reserve that protects only one stage of this movement may deliver limited benefits. Scientists use tracking, habitat maps, and current models to identify connected areas. Size also matters: very small zones are easier to patrol but may not contain enough habitat for wide-ranging species to remain protected.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph focuses on selecting effective boundaries by considering movement, habitat connections, and size.

paragraphs.text: Rules have little effect when coastal communities see them as unfair or impossible to follow. Fishers may possess detailed knowledge of seasonal conditions, and involving them early can improve both boundaries and permitted activities. Local monitoring groups also report violations more quickly than distant agencies. Participation does not eliminate conflict, since restrictions can reduce income in the short term. It does, however, make enforcement more credible because the reasons for protection and the process for changing rules are better understood.
paragraphs.explanation: The main purpose is to show how community participation strengthens legitimacy and enforcement.

paragraphs.text: Protection must be evaluated after a reserve is established. Researchers repeatedly measure fish abundance, habitat condition, and fishing activity inside and outside the boundary. If target species do not recover, managers examine whether the cause is illegal fishing, poor placement, pollution, or simply insufficient time. Regulations can then be adjusted rather than defended unchanged. This cycle of monitoring and revision treats a protected area as an ongoing management process, not a one-time decision that is automatically successful.
paragraphs.explanation: The paragraph describes repeated monitoring and the use of evidence to revise protection strategies.
